content.assessment: Reforming admissions while preserving track structure leaves the core psychological mechanisms of selection largely intact: the same number of sorting points and reference-group transitions remain, so big-fish-little-pond dynamics and any labeling effects tied to track placement are not directly addressed by this proposal. The mechanism targets who gets selected (via reducing SES-linked preparation advantages) rather than how selection itself psychologically affects selected and non-selected students. To the extent it succeeds in changing the composition of who is selected, it could marginally alter which students experience reference-group effects, but the proposal's own risk section acknowledges that self-exclusion is driven by parental strategic behavior rather than exam design, suggesting weak leverage over the actual psychological drivers of participation and aspiration.
opportunities: If admissions reform genuinely broadens the applicant pool, it could reduce concentration of disadvantage in comprehensive schools, indirectly moderating some big-fish-little-pond costs for those newly gaining access
strengths: Could reduce SES-correlated self-exclusion patterns if preparation-access barriers are successfully lowered, potentially expanding aspiration among previously excluded groups
threats: Partial reform limited to admissions process may generate symbolic rather than substantive change in student composition, leaving underlying motivational and self-concept dynamics unchanged despite perceived reform
weaknesses: Does not alter the number or timing of selection points, leaving reference-group and self-concept dynamics associated with the existing track structure unaddressed
Self-exclusion is described as driven by parental strategic decisions rather than exam design, suggesting limited causal leverage over the psychological/motivational drivers of non-participation
